rows.reporting: Stichting Agriterra
rows.title: providing financial services to the poor
rows.description:
This is a description of the two main activities/programmes that will be supported by Agriterra in 2012: 

Mutual Aid Services (micro-insurance)
NEFSCUN Mutual Aid is a co-operative based financial security system. It is aimed at pooling resources among members those are excluded from formal social protection mechanism under co-operative principles & values. In 2012 we plan to expand services reaching bigger SACCOS, and having sold over 5000 policies reaching breakeven point. CSP policies, in 2013 this will be 7700and in 2014 this must be 9700 policies. We will do this by providing active follow-up by visiting those SACCOS that have received orientation, expressed interest in providing CSP to their members, and focusing the programme in the bigger saccos who have larger number of members to this date already 88 SACCOS signed the MoC (memorandum of Cooperation) and 66 SACCO started selling and/or sales promotion. CSP will be promoted as complementary service to traditional savings and credit services and part of the financial solutions SACCOS can provide to their members. 

Microfinance 
NEFSCUN’s microfinance is based upon Credit Union Microfinance Innovation (CUMI) method. Which aims to provide sustainable financial services to the poor by promotion of savings, provision of affordable terms of loans, entrepreneurship development training and providing general credit union financial solutions to the poor.
Focused on the long-term well-being CUMI offers many advantages to the low-income poor, integrating low-income members into the mainstream of the society by contribution to their wealth building capacity. CUMI is the component in Micro finance services focusing on Women Entrepreneurs within the CUMI/microfinance groups. By the end of 2012 we want to enroll a cumulative 100,000 low income members, mainly women. This means an additional 24.380 (90% Female) new members for 2012.

Our core services are: 
1. Central Finance Facility Interlending (CFFI), 
2. Training and Education
3. NEFSCUN Mutual Aid (‘micro-insurance’), 
4. Stationery services.
5. Cooperative Banking Software Solutions
6. Microfinance services

CFFI was initiated in 1996 and its objective is to provide share, savings and loan facilities to member SACCOS. Through Training and education services NEFSCUN aims to strengthen the capacity of SACCO's staff and leaders which must lead to improvement of performance of the SACCOS operations. 

NEFSCUN Mutual Aid is a co-operative based financial security system. It is aimed at pooling resources among low-income members, who are excluded from formal social protection mechanism, under co-operative principles & values. Through our stationary services we provide all required materials for registration of SACCOS data as well as educational publications and posters, by ordering stationary centralize we can provide materials at reduced rate to our members. Cooperative Banking Software Solutions aims to assist SACCOS in digitalizing their operations and increasing efficiency in serving their members with up-to date account data, software also serves to provide fast and up-to date statements and management reports. Since most of the individual members are low-income people there is a big demand for microfinance services, we promote and capacitate the SACCOS to provide these services to existing and new members on the basis of the CUMI methodology.   

In September 2009 NEFSCUN committed itself, in front of the annual Forum of the Association of Asian Confederation of Credit Unions (ACCU), to reach, in the period from 2010-2012, 100,000 low income people (of which 80% women) with our microfinance services and contribute to building their wealth. We plan to do this by providing microfinance services applying the Credit Union Microfinance Innovations (CUMI) methodology (for more about CUMI methodology, see box under chapter 3, Objective 1). In 2012 we plan to provide CUMI based microfinance services to 25000 new members.    

Apart from our core business NEFSCUN is working in the following fields: advocacy & networking, policy support, Microfinance and business development services, ACCESS branding, financial intermediation, alternative energy promotion and organizing its resources to become a recognized information center for savings and Credit Cooperatives in Nepal.

We are putting our utmost efforts to Credit Union development in Nepal. We realize that there remains much work to be done. We have ambitious objectives for ourselves and our members. These include: providing additional services, capacity strengthening, diversifying our existing products & services, strengthening monitoring & supervision, implement computerized grading system and increasing our membership base. Our goals are ambitious but with the help of our members & development partners we know we can achieve them. 

Agriterra is requested to support especially the mutual aid and CUMI programme in 2012 (as in previous years)

Expanding Mutual Aid services and reaching break even point in this business and expanding microfinance services to a total of 100.000 women.

rows.reporting: Stichting Agriterra
rows.title: Improving production and market access in Dhading cooperatives
rows.description:
The Small Farmer Agriculture Cooperative Federation (SFACF) in Dhading district was established in 1993, it is an umbrella organization affiliating 37 Small Farmer Agricultural Cooperatives Limited (SFACL), one per Villa Development Committee (VDC). The cooperative has 20748 shareholders (11.535 women). 

In Dhading district  there exist 50 VDC from which until now 37 are covered by SFACF, and within this project SFACF aims to reach 5 VDC of the 13 remaining.  In the 5 VDC will be established 250 new groups and 5  new cooperatives, one per VDC. The establishment of the groups will directly increase the membership of SFACF with 5 new cooperatives and at least  1750 women farmers. Another objective of the project is to increase the income of the farmers by improving the production, increasing the skill of the farmers in entrepreneurship management and getting access to the markets. 
Youth and women farmers will be central in the project, creating opportunities for them through the different trainings planned and the establisment of cooperatives.

Expected results
1. Small farmer communities are organized and are capable making  informed choices and actively participate to optimize their livelihood. 
2. 80% of the participants trained will be employed by their cooperatives
3. farmers increase their incomes by  33% from Rs 1500 to Rs 2000 per month. 
4. increase in the production and farmers fulfill market demands. 
4. increase in the access of farmers to micro credit  
4. farmers products are sold through the collection center property of the cooperative at a favorable price 
5. Improved relationship between farmer and buyer. 
6. Both newly formed and existing cooperatives are well managed 

In order to achieve these results the following activities will be performed:

a.	For the new cooperatives the following trainings will be performed:  account keeping, leadership, cooperative education and registration training, management of savings and credit training.
13 support staff will be enrolled,  one per new cooperative
b.	In order to improve entrepreneurship skills of youth farmers: training on wiring, animal heath, technical aspects of agriculture, goat raising
c.	to improve the production of vegetables and goats: acquisition of  equipment for agro vet services (microscope will be purchased and given to 10 cooperatives), a buck , improved seeds, micro-irrigation,
d.	To improve the access to market: three collection centers will be set up.

The direct participants of the project will be 1920 farmers from which 95% will be women (1832) at the local level. 

At least 250 groups will participate in the activities of the project , and representatives of 19 cooperatives. 

The total budget for this project amounts to €65.440, €5.049 will be contribution of SFACF, €5.975 contribution of the community and €54.416 is requested to Agriterra.

rows.reporting: Stichting Agriterra
rows.title: grassroot entrepreneurship and capacity building of cooperatives and CTCF
rows.description:
CTCF is a client of Agriterra since 2012. Initially supported by the team advocacy, but realising the contribution towards the results of the team grass root entrepreneurship, it was decided to make CTCF a client of the team grass root entrpreneurship from 2013 onwards. 

The vision of CTCF is towards economic development through tea cooperative promotion and building the capacity of the memebrs of the cooperatives. CTCF works on a central level to improve the situation of the cooperatives. At the grassroots level, CTCF conducts various trainings programmes and provided continously coaching and support through its facilitators to the primary cooperatives and its members.  

CTCF has currently 66 member tea cooperatives from Ilam, Panchthar, Dhankuta, Therathum, Jhapa, Udayapur, Bhojpur and Lalitpur districts including four district cooperative federations, with more than 3,972 tea farmers associated through their cooperatives. The tea farmers as members of cooperatives are gradually increasing and the number of cooperatives is expected to be more than 72, with more than 4,300 farmers as members, by the end of 2013.

Update at the beginning of 2014: 
CTCF has currently 73 member tea cooperatives from Ilam, Panchthar, Dhankuta, Therathum, Jhapa, Udayapur, Bhojpur and Lalitpur districts including five district cooperative federations, with more than 4699 tea farmers associated through their cooperatives. The tea farmers as members of cooperatives are gradually increasing and the number of cooperatives is expected to be grow gradually.

Overall goal is to contribute in sustained rural poverty reduction of smallholder tea farmers of Eastern districts of Nepal by increasing their income, and equally empower small farmer based organisations to provide better technical and management services.

Objectives: Key objectives of the project:
1.	Capacity building Entrepreneurship skills development and marketing support services to the tea entrepreneurs
2.	Support tea cooperatives and farmers for Access to Finance and capacity building support services on  financials
3.	Lobby and advocacy on various tea issues (favourable tea policy, celebration of 16th National Tea Day , organic inputs, certification, collateral free loan etc.) 
4.	To establish effective supervision and monitoring system in tea sub sector for regular dissemination of information.

Objectives for 2014 are more or less same as in 2013, but slightly more focused: 

1.	Capacity building of tea cooperatives and farmers in their institutional building, resource mobilization, business planning etc. 
2.	Enhance entrepreneurship and business skills of STPUs operated by cooperatives.
3.	Lobby and advocacy works on various tea related issues faced by the tea farmers. 
4.	Regular monitoring and supervision support to all tea stakeholders.


Expected results:

1.	Minimum of ten (10) cooperatives will be able to start Mini Tea Processing (MTP) units after receiving technical inputs, entrepreneurship trainings and other business support services including marketing. 
2.	MTP units will produce and sell 9.000 kg quality tea in the competitive markets.
3.	3 Cooperatives and individual tea farmers will be able to avail minimum 10 million loan amount easily at minimum interest rates.
4.	12 cooperatives will prepare business plan and start to business activities like saving credit, nurseling, tea planting, green leaf tea collection etc.
5.	7 cooperatives will review their business plan and prepare further plan. 
6.	Organic inputs such as fertilizers and pesticides will be made available to the tea farmers with organic tea garden at subsidized rates.
7.	Financial monitoring system (PEARL, Software, etc.,)  will be set-up in 9 cooperatives 
8.	FACT concept will be widely disseminated to a larger number of cooperatives and will internalize in their regular system.
9. 6 new cooperatives will take memebrship of CTCF
10. 10 young/weak coopertiaves willreceive coop leadership and management training

Period: From January 2013 until December 2013, but the project will in case of smooth implementation be extended for the year 2014, Activities and logframe are only for 2013. in the logframe, long term objectives are mentioned which can only be achieved in multiple years. 
Total Budget: Nrs. 6,142,100

Expected results 2014:

a.	50% of cooperatives will be able to generate resources on their own and sustain in a long run.
b.	30% of STPUs will be capable to produce quality tea as per the tea standard and managed to sell in the competitive market.
c.	There will be improvements in quality of green leaves produced by tea farmers. 5% of the tea farmers will adopt IPM or organic tea farming technology.
d.	Government and other stakeholders will be positive towards tea sector and there will be more investments to promote this sector. Some level changes will be visibly seen in 3 key areas. 
e.	Updated database on key tea indicators (production, sales, exports, number of farmers/cooperatives, etc.)

Coverage and target beneficiary:

Program will be implemented mainly in nine districts: Ilam, Panchthar, Dhankuta, Therathum, Lalitpur, and Bojpur targeting 5 district federations, 72 tea cooperatives, and about 4,500 tea farmers. This year, services will be extended in additional two districts (Lalitpur, Bojpur, Udayapur, Sankuwasava, Jhapa). In Lalitpur district, there are 8 tea cooperatives, District Tea Cooperative Federation (DTCF), and more than 225 members. Similarly, in Bojpur, Sankuwasava, Udayapur and Jhapa districts there are 7 cooperatives and more than 325 tea farmers.

Overall goal is to contribute in sustained rural poverty reduction of smallholder tea farmers of Eastern districts of Nepal by increasing their income, and equally empower small farmer based organisations to provide better technical and management services.
 
Objectives: Key objectives of the project:
 1. Entrepreneurship skills development and marketing support services to the tea entrepreneurs
 2. Support tea cooperatives and farmers for Access to Finance and capacity building support services on financials
 3. Lobby and advocacy on various tea issues (favourable tea policy, celebration of 16th National Tea Day , organic inputs, certification, collateral free loan etc.,) 
4. Advisory services on cooperative management and administration, and seeking experts from Agripool for support in developing marketing strategies for the members
 5. To establish effective supervision and monitoring system in tea sub sector for regular dissemination of information.

rows.title: Improving economic conditions through value chain enhancement and small farmers agricultural cooperatives
rows.description:
Project proposal received on 21 May 2013:
NACCFL has its roots in the Small Farmers Development Program which was implemented by the Agricultural Development Bank in 1975 for the development of the small and marginal destitute in rural areas. The sub project office became a Small Agriculture Cooperative Ltd (SFCL) in 1993 moment in which the ownership of the project was handed over to the farmers. In 2008 the farmers felt that they were facing different problems which could be better addressed by a federation and formed the NACCFL in January 2008. Eight district federations triggered its establishment.
This is a umbrella organization of Small Farmer Agro Cooperative Limited (SFACL). SFACL are the agriculture microfinance cooperatives (credit for entrepreneurship, i.e investment in agricultural production, transformation and commercialization) which are fully owned and managed by the local community. At the moment 467 SFACL are member of NACCFL. 
NACCF provide services to SFACL and the SFACL is responsible to provide the services to the groups and cooperatives.

The Central Federation is focused on the provision of non-financial services to their members, the services are provided through programs, focusing on capacity building, policy advocacy and co-operative network expansion.

The mission of the organisation is: 
To provide appropriate non-financial services to the member organization for their institutional development and for the socio-economic development of the small farmer and deprived class people.

Three objectives aims to achieve:
•	To improve the efficiency and autonomy of the members cooperatives through capacity building.
•	To advocate for the inclusion of deprived sector in the national budget
•	To increase its membership through the establishment of new cooperatives.

Agriterra came into contact with NACCFL through the SFACL Dhading (cooperation since mid 2012) in December 2012. The organisation passed the quick scan and was invited to submit a proposal. In April 2013, the LO visited NACCFL and Makawanpur district, to better understand how NACCFL works and interacts with the district and local level.). The way of working is very similar to the PFEIP approach promoted by team 2: individual farmers are organised into into small producer groups, and focus on one particular value chain to improve production and marketing of the product. The producer groups become member of the VDC level cooperative (vertical linkages and through this enhance the opportunities for collective access to finance, services and marketing. The SFACL are members of the District level SFACL. The national level federation is supporting all the districts that are members  and is being steered by the small scale farmer. 

Main target group are women farmers, as they have proven to be more reliable and serious implementers of the activities as individuals but also in groups. 

What are the goals, expected output and impact? 
Membership increase: yes
Increase of number of organized local groups:  yes, with 370 new groups
Economic initiatives aimed at market access or production enhancement:yes
Clustering (horizontal) (collaboration between producer groups): yes, through FFS and by creating SFACL at VDC level
Clustering (vertical) (collaboration with other chain actors): yes, the SFACL become member of the Dhading SFACL. 
Establishing primary co-operatives (business planning): yes

APPLICATIE - STEP 2

WA 4 supported by WA 1 

Estimated outreach?
Direct  beneficiaries (add individual no): 4297 at local level
-	Men: 250
-	Women: 4047

Local and sub national level outreach (tick mark only):
Local:X

National: only monitoring role
Number of local groups involved: (add no)It is expected that 399 existing groups will be further strengthened in their economic activities, and 330 new groups will be established. The groups comprise 6-7 persons on average. So total 729 Groups can be reached

Nepal Agriculture Cooperative Central Federation Ltd. (NACCFL) is the umbrella organization of Small Farmer Cooperative Limited (SFCL) and similar other agriculture cooperatives. SFCLs are agricultural microfinance cooperatives which are fully owned and managed by the local community. They have their origin in the Small Farmers Develop¬ment Program (SFDP) which had been implemented by the Agricultural Development Bank Ltd. (ADBL) in1975.
	
As of Aug 2012, local communities in 404 VDCs have formed their own SFCLs spread in 60 districts throughout the country. Among these SFCLs, 11 SFCLs have been performing limited banking operations with the permission of Nepal Rastra Bank (NRB). The formation of the NACCFL was based on the felt need of all local federations to have a national representation and followed a bottom-up approach. 
Vision:
We want to emerge as a leading and capable federation for the self-sufficiency and sustainable development of small farmer run small farmer Agriculture co-operatives and other co-operatives of similar nature so as to reduce existing poverty.

Mission:
We want to provide appropriate non-financial services to the member organizations for their institutional development and for the socio-economic development of the small farmers and deprived class people.

3. Problem Statement and Objectives:

Small farmer’s cooperatives also face challenges in producing above subsistence levels as well as accessing markets for commercialization. With the inability to access markets, many of the cooperative members are forced to sell through middlemen around the country and receive less than adequate prices for the surplus produce. Given that the majority of the producers in Nepal are subsistence oriented, there is a need to support agriculture commercialization where possible. Support is needed for enhancing the scale of production, post-harvest operations, processing and trade. There is a need to link farmers to markets and develop each step in the value chain for various commodities that will eventually connect with domestic, the Indian market, and with other international (niche) markets.

Cooperative development completed five decades in Nepal. There are more than 2700 cooperatives societies are in existence in different forms. Despite the existence of cooperatives for more than five decades, these organizations could not meet the objective of cooperatives to promote agriculture activities such as avail seed, agriculture equipment, seed money and appropriate technology and commercialize the agro product. Regular profit and proper utilization of the profit is assurance of sustainability. But unfortunately, most of cooperatives in rural areas fail to adopt this modern concept. They still depend upon grants rather than self dependency.

Although  the farmers are involved in vegetable, dairy, meat , honey and spices production they are not getting good price compared to the real market price due to low access to  proper market and its knowledge Similarly there were abundant bio resources such as butter tree and sugar free potato nearby forest, these are not in use. If farmers get appropriate technology for processing the same, there income generating activities will be increased. Proposed project will be helpful to address the problem by achieving its objectives that is increased income and capabilities of SFACLs engaged in production, processing and marketing on a sustainable way.

Objectives: The main objective of the project is to improve the living condition of rural population through smallholder value chain development in order to provide solutions that increase the income and capabilities of Small Farmers Agricultural Cooperatives (SFACL) engaged in production, processing and marketing on a sustainable basis. 

Sub objectives are:
1. Agriculture Market Development – The aim of this component is to enhance the profitability of SFCL members by improving their access to markets and their capacity to add value to locally produced raw materials. This will be achieved by (a) facilitating the linkage of SFCLs to local and national markets and (b) easing critical market infrastructure constraints to the processing, storage, handling and marketing of farm produced and their processed products.

2.SFCL Productivity Enhancement -The aim of this component is to enhance the productivity of SFACL member by providing various capacity developments training on production technology. 

3.Organizational Strengthening of the groups and cooperatives - The aim of this component is to strengthen the SFACL  organization by providing technical backstopping on the various components of farmers to farmers replication process.

The project area are 10 VDC in makwanpur area of which 5 new VDC to replicate the model to create groups and SFACl. teh other 5 VDC teh replication process is already started. All areas are in teh more remote, hilly.mounteneuaos part of teh district. pre identified valuechains are: henoy, milk, seeds and goat raising. 

Activities:

-Trainings
4 trainings on production enhancement
10 Farmer field Schools
8 trainings on marketing and processing
8 training related to organsational strengtening and , leadership , bsuiness plan preparation etc. (for details see the proposal) 
- support in group establishment, intergroup establishment and coopertaive establishment
- Collection of baseline information through surevy (at teh beginning of the project) 
- exposure visits
- Marketing support (trade exhibition, MIS training)
- Monitoring and Evaluation by NACCFL

 the new groups are supported by group prmotors/ (social mobilizers) in the group formation process. The social mobilizers are guided by two facilitator or replicators, who will further coach the promoters to properly guide and coach the groups and coopertaives (10) and also faciliate /organise the trainings. The promotors might become the managers of the SFACL in the future. 

Expected results:

370 new groups established, 399 existing groups strengthened
Outreach is around 4300 persons, 92% are women.
10 new SFAC established
Increase in productivity, increase in market sales, increase in income
The SFACL are eligable to take soft loans from the Sana Kisan development Bank

rows.reporting: Stichting Agriterra
rows.title: Strengthen Agricultural Cooperatives and support commercial farmers
rows.description:
The Small Farmer Agriculture Cooperative Federation (SFACF) in Dhading district was established in 1993. I, it is an umbrella organization affiliating 37 Small Farmer Agricultural Cooperatives Limited (SFACL), one per Villa Development Committee (VDC). The cooperative has 20748 shareholders (11.535 women). Dhading district  covers 50 VDC from which until now 37 are covered by SFACF. 
SFACF Dhading is a member of the Nepal Agricultural Cooperatives Central Federation (NACCFL). Main aim of NACCFL is to support small farmers to improve their livelihood. Through the formation of cooperatives, initially focussing on saving and credit, small farmers get access to the small farmers agricultural development bank (SKBB). The cooperative members can take loans under fair conditions to invest in agriculture. The SFACL are shaerholder of SKB Bank. NACCFL has  developed a replication strategy that aims to involve those VDC where small farmers cooperatives do not exist yet . Under this strategy, groups, intergroups and finally a VDC level cooperative will be formed. Once the cooperative is meeting the criteria to be handed over to the bank, members have access to Agricultural loans.

Main objective is to increase the income of the farmers by improving the production, increasing the skills of the farmers in entrepreneurship management and getting access to the markets. 

SFACF Dhading aims to reach 12 VDCs in Dhading district in particular: 6 VDCs in which the replication process of group, intergroup and cooperative establishment started last year (project 5905) and 2 VDC in which the replication will start this year; and 4 VDC in which the cooperatives are already functioning but where specific support will be provided to stimulate commercial farming. Focus of this project is to improve the agricultural productivity and support small farmers producing for the market by providing additional  capacity building and technical support through local social mobilisers.

At the end of the project it is expected that 6 cooperatives are formally registered cooperatives and handed over to the small farmers development bank (SKKB), and thus have access to finance. 
The total number of new groups formed will be 100 and 250 groups continue to receive technical support from SFACF Dading with an exclusive focus on improved productivity and access to markets for four selected Agricultural commodities: dairy(milk), goat (meat), coffee and vegetables. Several training and extension programmes will be implemented related to these four commodities, such as Farmer Field schools, technical trainings, marketing trainings etc. 
Attention is paid to further strengthening the primary cooperatives and the groups. This involves trainings reated to leadership, financial management, cooperative management etc. 

The establishment of the groups will directly increase the membership of SFACF with 6 new cooperatives and at least  1750 women farmers. 

This project is a continuation of project 5905, the first year of cooperation between SFACF Dhading and Agriterra. Main results of the first year projects is that in 6 new VDC the process of establishing Agricultural cooperatives has been initiated and  alot of capacity building took place focusing on improving the productivity and income generating capacities of the members . Focus was especially on youth and women, providing opportunities to have accesto loans and skill improvemnet related to several income generating activities.

Location of the Project Activities	Dhading district of Nepal

Goal
The overall goal of the proposed project is to "improve the economic status of farmers through  improved agricultural practices, access to the market and finance."

The objectives of the project are as follows: 
1.	Strengthen new and existing cooperatives
2.	Increase the agricultural production of cooperative members
3.	Youth community and cooperatives members serve SF members with technical and entrepreneurial skills and knowledge.
4.	Facilitate access to the market.
5.	Strengthen the capacity of SFACF Dhading.


Project Outputs
1.  Two new SFACL, will be formed and 6 replication VDCs further strengthened and handed over to Small farmers development bank.
2. Cooperative members will have improved managerial skills so that newly formed and existing cooperatives will be managed effectively.
3.  Improved production (quality and quantity) of four selected commodities by commercial/lead  farmers of groups, resulting in better income
4. Commercial farmers sell their products in the market either individualy or through the cooperatives.
5. Board and staff of SFACF Dhading have improved their management skills and SFACF is able to provide better quality services to its members.


Budget	Total Budget:€ 110,162
	Agriterra Contribution: € 96,587
	SFACF and Community contribution: €13,574

Selected value chains are : dairy /milk; goat; vegetables and coffee.

see project proposal for detailed description of all activities.

rows.title: Strengthening Planning, Monitoring, Evaluation and Learning
rows.description:
NACCFL has its roots in the Small Farmers Development Program which was implemented by the Agricultural Development Bank in 1975 for the development of the small and marginal destitute in rural areas. The sub project office became a Small Agriculture Cooperative Ltd (SFCL) in 1993, the moment in which the ownership of the project was handed over to the farmers. In 2008 the farmers felt that they were facing different problems which could be better addressed by a federation and formed the NACCFL in January 2008. NACCFL is an umbrella organization of various Small Farmer Agro Cooperative Limited (SFACL). At the moment 615 SFACL are member of NACCFL in 64 different districts across the country covering 530,000 individual farmer members. NACCF provide services to SFACL and the SFACL is responsible to provide the services to the groups and cooperatives.

The mission of NACCFL is to provide appropriate non-financial services to the member organizations for their institutional development and for the socio-economic development of the small farmers and deprived people. The three objectives aim to (1) to improve the efficiency and autonomy of the members cooperatives through capacity building; (2) to advocate for the inclusion of deprived sector in the national budget and (3) to increase its membership through the establishment of new cooperatives.

In addition to the projects focussing on farmer-led economic development, Agriterra and NACCFL took the initiative mid-2014 to collaborate in a separate project to strengthen and improve the NACCFL-PMEL system and tools. This trajectory starts with and is supported by the expertise of an external local PMEL-expert.

Based on the M&E health check, a M&E SWOT analysis and the M&E workshop held in Manila in March 2014, the following can be concluded about the M&E capacity of NACCFL: 

- There is a will to do M&E but a lack of human resources to conduct systematic M&E at all levels of NACCFL. 
- There is a membership database software system designed but not yet operational. NACCFL is keen to make this membership data base operation as registration of members and their characteristics is crucial to further improve service provision to members. 
- In general, NACCFL prepares plans and budget but M&E is not systematically addressed, good indicators are missing and there is a lack of information of te members at the individual level or group level.

From the above analyses and taking into account the context described and the development stage and mission of NACCFL, both NACCFL and Agriterra agreed upon the following priorities related to strengthening the M&E capacities and system of  NACCFL for the period August 2014 to December 31, 2014 -->   

A. Database: development of a membership registration database maintained by, owned and supported at both the local, district and national level. 

B. M&E on Agricultural activities: the developed membership registration database should include agricultural details/characteristics of the members. These data should be directly linked with the member registration for as such it allows for a comprehensive M&E system focusing on agriculture. 

NACCFL has requested the support of Agriterra to make their current ACCESS-system operational. This requires two approaches:

1. Datacollection at grass root level be done (what data must be collected, how, how often and by whom).
2. What data are need at the national level, for what purpose and how to make the database operational  (design, responsibility and consolidation of data).

Concerning the process 
To ensure data collection and absorption, it is important to have the commitment of members/groups at all levels. Commitment is linked to social, economic and intrinsic factors. The intrinsic factor can be stimulated by asking only the relevant data, and make it clear and understandable why the data are needed and what they mean (the feedback). The social factor is about trust and loyalty among the members. A transparent way of working, involving the local people can ensure openness and commitment to provide the data and to collect the data. With economic factors we refer to economic incentives that motivate people to collect the data. Not necessarily salaries, but a small remuneration for the SFACFL staff that compile the data.

Agriterra is willing to support NACCFL to make the membership database system operation and ensure feeding the system with relevant agricultural information important for the members and the organisations itself. In the detailed action plan and budget, the most important steps (actions) are described. A local advisor with expertise and experience in the field of M&E and membership registration will support NACCFL for the period of August to December. NACCFL has the capacity and committment available to adhere to this M&E trajectory.

From the above analyses and taking into account the context described in paragraph 1 and the development stage and mission of NACCFL we (NACCFL and Agriterra) agree upon the following priorities related to strengthening the M&E capacities and system of  NACCFL:  

I. Membership registration: further development of a membership registration database maintained by, owned and supported at both the local, district and national level. 

II. M&E on Agricultural activities: the developed membership registration database should include agricultural details/characteristics of the members. These data should be directly linked with the member registration for as such it allows for a comprehensive M&E system focusing on agriculture. 

The trajectory entails the remaining of 2014, August 2014 to December 31, 2014. The details of the priority areas are worked out into the action plan, see paragraph 6. NACCFL has requested the support of Agriterra to make their current ACCESS-system operational. This requires two approaches:

1. Data collection at grass root level be done (what data must be collected, how, how often and by whom).

2. What data are need at the national level, for what purpose and how to make the database operational  (design, responsibility and consolidation of data).

rows.reporting: Stichting Agriterra
rows.title: provide immediate/emergency  response  for the earthquake affected members of NACCFL
rows.description:
On April 25, 2015 at 11: 56 AM, Nepal was shocked by powerful earthquake with magnitude of 7.9 Richter scale, affecting the central part of the country mostly. The impact is huge in 14 districts of te country as well as in the capital city. Agriterra and its constituency decided that such a natural disaster can not be neglected as it is has a major impact upon the people's  lifes. Among the NACCFL members, about 700 persons died; 87% of all rural families lost their house, or have serious damaged houses; several lost their livestock, animal sheds, seeds etc. After receiving the first relief support, the first priority for people is to rebuild their houses. After that it is important that people pick up/continue their agricultural business as this is their main income and food source.
 
NACCFL is receiving huge demand on for the emergency response from its member’s cooperative for earthquake victims. The need is shelter, primary health services and income generating activities. 

Agriterra knows that the farmers organisation are in a very good position to provide relief/reconstruction support, having an extensive network reaching out to the most remote areas of the country. 

The Nepal Agriculture Co-operative Central Federation Ltd. (NACCF) is the umbrella organization of Small Farmer Agro Cooperative Limited (SFACL) . SFACL are agricultural micro-finance cooperatives, which are fully owned and managed by the local community. They have their origin in the Small Farmers Development Program (SFDP), which has been implemented by the Agricultural Development Bank Ltd. (ADBL) since 1975. It is specifically designed for small and marginal farmers. AT present 611 SFACL in 68 districts are member of NACCFL, representing 615.000 individual members. 

SFACLs face different  problems, such as gaining access to markets, acquiring new technical and management skills, obtaining loan and credit services, etc. Subsequently, they decided to fight poverty by federating their cooperatives into a strong network. 

NACCFL mission is to provide appropriate non-financial services to the member organization for their institutional development and for the socio-economic development of the small farmers.

NACCFL was requested by Agriterra to submit a proposal focusing on reconstruction of agricultural activities in few of the most affected areas where they trust the capacity of the District Agricultural Federation. 

Strongly affected districts Dhading and Makwanpur will receive relief and reconstruction support directly from Agriterra for those SFACL that Agriterra has been working with the last 2-3 years (see also projects 6362 and 6363) . The other SFACL in these two districts will receive relief and reconstruction support through NACCFL, as the capacity of the District Agricultural Federation is not enough to support all SFACL.

In addition, NACCFl proposes to support 3 SFACL in Rasuwa district and two SFACL in Nuwakot district,  not far from each other (cluster) at about two hours drive from Kathmandu.

In total 21 SFACL (primary cooperatives) will benefit from the support.

95 percent member SFACLs in the highly affected districts lost their house and now they are residing in either tent or temporary shelter. In addition to that, domestic animal also died as the communities were fully depended to livestock and its product for their livelihood. The program will be implemented through members SFACL of NACCFL. 95 % of member is earthquake affected members among them 82 % are female members.  In this program, 8186 members that is 52% of the affected members of proposed district will be benefited.

In one hand they lost their house, in another hand they do not have alternatives for income generating activities. To carry out the agricultural activities as per agro calendar in some of the farmers nether they have Ox for plough the land as Ox were also died nor they have seeds.

In the five proposed VDCs of Nuwakot and Rasuwa districts, the earthquake has destroyed/hampered all the sources of livelihood and other economic activities. Members were engaged in agriculture, poultry and livestock activities. These were primary sources of income for them to contribute to family expenses.  

The SFACL is selected on the basis of following criteria:
a.	Highly affected SFACL
b.	VDCs mentioned in Dhading and Makwanpur districts are not included in the relief program of   District Agricultural Cooperative Federation (DAF ) of Dhading and Makwanpur.
c.	Remote SFACL
d.	Majority of people are disadvantage group
e.	Highly depended on agriculture sector
f.	Lost both cattle and  house
g.	Majority of the members are female.


Proposed activities are:
-Distribution of rice and wheat seeds
-Distribution of plants and seedlings for horticulture
-Distribution of goats and piglets
-Construction of livestock sheds

The support will be equaly distributed among teh 21 coopertiaves. 

NACCFL is the final responisible organisation for the administration and monitoring of the support program. As much responsibility as possible will be delegated to the district Federation and the primary coops. 

Total budget requested to Agriterra is 40,000 Euro. 
Own contribution of NACCFL is: 14.587 Euro

Total budget: 54.587 Euro

Total number of beneficiaries: 780 members of NACCFL benefitting an estimated 11.580 people.

to provide immediate/emergency response for the earthquake effected members of 21 Small Farmer Agriculture Cooperative Limited (SFACL) of 4 districts within  NACCFL network.".

rows.title: Relief and reconstruction support to Agricultural cooperatives in Dhading
rows.description:
On April 25, 2015 at 11: 56 AM, Nepal was shocked by a powerful earthquake with magnitude of 7.9 Richter scale, affecting the central part of the country mostly. The impact is huge in 14 districts of te country as well as in the capital city. Agriterra and its constituency decided that such a natural disaster can not be necklecked as it is has a major impact upon the people's  lifes. In Dhading district, 728 persons died and 3383 are injured; 59% of all houses are fully destroyed; and 39% of all houses are partly destroyed. Families lost their livestock, animal sheds, seeds etc. After receiving the first relief support, the first priority for people is to rebuild their houses. After that it is important that people pick up/continue their agricultural business as this is their main income and food source.
 
Agriterra knows that the farmers organisation are in a very good position to provide relief/reconstruction support, having an extensive network reaching out to the most remote areas of the country. 

Agriterra has a cooperation with the District Agricultural Federation of Dhading since 2012 focussing on promoting farmer entreprenuership and strengthening of the primary agricultural cooperatives in 8 Village Development Committees (VDC) . 

This project aims to support the most affected farmer families in those 8 VDCs in the (partial) reconstruction of their homes and agricultural activities. The offices of the primary cooperatives (SFACFL), their central meeting point for the saving and cerdit, meetings etc are also severely damage and most important maintenance will be done.

The reconstruction supports includes the following activities for the members of the Agricultural cooperatives in 8 VDCs: Gumdi, Marpak, Semjong, Katunje, Budhathum, Phulkharka, Sangkosh and Jogimara.


•	500 HH of SFACLs members will be benefited  for reconstruction of houses and cattle sheds (Roof material (JASTA) will be provided under this project, other cost will be covered by themselves)

•	350 HH of SFACLs members will be provided Cattle (Goat, Pig lets, chicken) support 
•	2600 HH of SFACLs member will be provided seeds of different vegetables
•	8 SFACLs offices will be repaired and well (furniture and utensil will be provided to the SFACLS) 

Total Budget in EURO: 68.855
Agriterra contribution in EURO	66414
SFACL contribution in Euro	2.441

Own contribution by the farmers (in labour, local construction materials etc: Euro 97.272

Total outreach: 2600 members of SFACL and their family members families

Total budget:

The major objective of this project is “Access of immediate relief support for the reform of the Agricultural Sector to earthquake victim in Makawanpur district”.

rows.reporting: Stichting Agriterra
rows.title: Reconstruction and relief support to farmer cooperatives in Makwanpur
rows.description:
On April 25, 2015 at 11: 56 AM, Nepal was shocked by powerful earthquake with magnitude of 7.9 Richter scale, affecting the central part of the country mostly. The impact is huge in 14 districts of te country as well as in the capital city. Agriterra and its constituency decided that such a natural disaster can not be neglected as it is has a major impact upon the people's  lifes. In total almost 9000 people were killed, over 17,000 peeple were injured, and at least 500,000 houses were destroyed. Makwanpur district is one of the 14 most hit districts. The District Agricultural Federation, having 25 Small farmers Agricultural cooperatives as member made an inventory of the damage among the members. 

Details of 10 cooperatives that Agriterra has been supporting over the last two years shows that out of 4141 member households
- 8 people died and 12 got injured
- 957 families completely lost their house/livestock sheds
- 680 families partially lost their house/lvestock shed
- loss of food stock in 5 cooperatives 
- loss of livestock 7 cooperatives. 

After receiving the first relief support, the first priority for people is to rebuild their houses. After that it is important that people pick up/continue their agricultural business as this is their main income and food source.
 
Makwanpur is one of the 14 severely affected districts in Nepal. Agriterra works since 2013 with the District Agricultural Federation of Makwanpur. District Agriculture co-operative Federation was established in 2010 to work as an umbrella organization for the primary Agricultural cooperatives. Vision of the federation is to have a capable and transparent district level umbrella organization providing services to small scale agriculture co-operatives and related co-operatives in helping them economically, socially, culturally, and with technology based agricultural production, purification and marketing. 

Project 6016 (contracted through NACCFL but implemented by DAF makwanpur) describes how Agriterra has been working with 10 primary cooperatives in Makwapur district.

The earthquake has severly damaged Makwanpur district including the 10 Village Development Commities in which Agriterra has been working actively. For these 10 VDC a relief and Agricultural reconstruction programme has been prepared. The support includes the following:
- blankets and kitchen utensils
- roofing matrials for house/animal shed reconstruction
- knapsack sprayer for vegetables
- vegetable seeds
- beehives in one VDC
- young animals in few VDC
 
Financial support will be channelled through the District Federation. The Federation has created a disaster risk response team (responsible for selection of recipients and distribution of the goods) and a procurement team (collective purchase of goods). 

The primary cooperatives and the district federation  will be responsible for the selection of the right beneficiaries, keep the records of the distribution of goods and provide technical support. Monitoring and evaluation of the support porgramme will be done by DAF, NACCFL and Agriterra.

The period for the project is 3 months; june-July and August 2015.

Beneficiaries: 4141 members, of which 470 members (Women only) receive support for reconstruction. Most families will receive seeds. 

Update September 2015: After the succesful distribution of different Agricultural inputs, and with the assage of time, farmers pick up their routine in farming again. Most families have foudn a temporarily housinga nd cultivate crops again. In ordre to furher support the farmesr in rebuidling their economic activities, this project will be extended till the end of 2015 to further strengteh the Agricultural reconstruction. DAF Makwanpur proposes several training activities, as well as s tronger focus on coopertaive marketing for those coopertiaves that have a fouvorable locationtowards the market. DAF Makwapnrur is very consious about teh sustainability of its activities and realises the importance of having farmer led advisory service sto teh memebrs. In this activity plan, lead farmers are the key persons in making a change and focusing really on commercial farming, bringing in a  more entrepreneurial spirit and attitude. 


Budget: 59,500 Euro (June-AUgust 2015 Phase 1) 
15,000 Euro (September-December 2015, Phase 2) 

Monitoring will be done by DAF and also by NACCFL and Agriterra. 
DAF will also receive donated high quality vegetable seeds and they will be distributed to selected farmers.

The major objective of this project is “Access of immediate relief support for the reform of the Agricultural Sector to earthquake victim in Makawanpur district”.

rows.reporting: Stichting Agriterra
rows.title: Sustainable member services and improved acces to public funds.
rows.description:
CTCF has two main objectives; sustainable services for members and effective lobby and advocacy for better representation and access to funds. This is clearly formulated in the stategic plan 2015-2018 of CTCF. It is expected that in the coming 2-3 years, CTCF will be able to fully cover its own overhead costs; provide quality services as a service provider to third parties (and generate an income from this), able to attract government support (either fianncially or services) for its members, and provide facilitating services to member cooperatives in the field of marketing of tea.

CTCF is gradually building its own financial base in order to become a sustainable service provider to its members and to represent the members well. This project aims to address the financially sustainability of CTCF, how an CTCF work towards cost neutral services, position itself better into the market as a service provider (paid by third parties) and ensuring higher member committment showing off in for example paying regular membership fees, own contribution for certain services and other ways of internal capitalisation. At the same time, quality of the requested services is important, and should be upgraded where possible. Advisory services by Agriterra will focus in improved sustainable service provision (like record keeping and bench marking, design a sustainable farmer led services system, cooperative management and governance, financial management, marketing related services and further institutionalisation of FACT, in order to improve lobby and advocacy by the Federation at both national, district and village level. 
This proposal includes a proposal for AgriCord funding making use of the FO funding facility for access to public grans/facilities. Main purpose is to get access to the Danisch Embassy Funding of UNNATI. This will be done by using the FACT methodology, already introduced to CTCF in 2015. The organisation is ready for wider role out of this methodology to involve more members at the district and local level in using this systematic approach. The AgriCord proposal is integrated in the whole activity plan, and a separate project note is added (oploaded file)

To address the financially sustainability of CTCF, how an CTCF work towards cost neutral services, position itself better into the market as a service provider (paid by third parties) and ensuring higher member committment showing off in for example paying regular membership fees, own contribution for certain services and other ways of internal capitalisation.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Dialogues on Sustainable Alternatives
rows.description:
SADED Nepal promotes grassroots democracy and ecologically sustainable society, using mainly the methodology of dialogues. The dialogues on sustainable alternatives will be carried out in particular on the following themes: youth and migration, biodiversit
y and farming, and sustainable rebuilding. The dialogues are facilitated on different levels. On a village level in Nuwakot, Rasuwa, Dhading, Sindhupalchowk, Dolakha, Dhankuta and Sankhuwakhabha districts the dialogues seek to be an impetus for a lasting e
mpowering platform. On a district or regional level dialogues take place between villagers and policy makers (wanting to challenge the usual top down policy making process), whilst the dialogues in the centre, in Kathmandu will reach out to students and na
tional policy and opinion makers. The dialogues are also continued at the Social Forum process and the dialogues will in total be feeding into a policy alternatives building process (forming national consortium for policy alternatives) between different ac
tors besides SADED, as trade unions and various youth groups, which will meet at regular intervals around the main themes of the dialogues. The outcomes of the dialogues, the ideas around sustainable alternatives, will be published and disseminated in the 
interconnected dialogue processes via newsletters, newspaper items, SADED's website (http://sadednepal.org.np) a journal and books.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Developing biocultural community protocols in Community Forestry in Nepal
rows.description:
Within the project members of five community forestry communities will be trained on the national and international legal context of biocultural community protocols. The same communities will document their biocultural life-heritage, develop biocultural co
mmunity protocols and organize local campaigns to demand bio-cultural rights. Representatives of FECOFUN and local communities will participate in the meetings of the UN Convention on Biological Diversity to share the experiences related to the community p
rotocols. Individual biocultural community protocols will be published at local level. In addition, FECOFUN will publish an evaluation report on the state of biocultural rights of local and indigenous communities to be shared at international fora. The bio
cultural rights of the indigenous and local communities have been recognized in many international human rights and biodiversity instruments, but because of the reluctance of the Nepalese government, they are not implemented. Community forestry plans based
 on the National Forest Act emphasize technical standards rather than rights, so it is necessary to produce separate biocultural protocols. At Community level knowledge of international instruments such as the UN Biodiversity Agreement is weak, so it is ne
cessary to increase it. Communities also need stronger campaigning capacity for biocultural rights to be included in forest management plans and forestry legislation. As a result of the project, the awareness of biocultural rights of local communities is e
xpected to grow, the communities' biocultural life heritage will be documented and biocultural protocols will be drafted, government and other actors will recognize the community's cultural rights and the campaigning capacity of the community will be stren
gthened to defend the rights. FECOFUN and local community representatives will be able to effectively participate in national and international forums and share the publication with wider audiences. The beneficiaries of the project are the members of the s
elected five Community Forestry Groups in the following districts: Bardiya (Tharu People, Khata Corridor), Sunsari (peasants, Ramdhuni Cultural Forest), Kathmandu (Tamang people, Shandong National Park Buffer Zone), Rasuwa / Nuwakot (Tamang People, Lantang
 National Park Buffer Zone), Dhading / Gorkha (various populations, Community Forest adjacent to the Budigandaki hydropower project). 'Biocultural Rights' is a concept related to the international

rows.reporting: Stichting Agriterra
rows.title: Defining the future strategy and improving financial management of JCCU
rows.description:
"The Junar Central Cooperative Union was established in 2008. It is a federation of the Sindhuli Junar District Union and the Ramechap Junar District Union, and 43 primary cooperatives, representing about 7626 individual farmers. JCCU has the mandate to advise and assist the district unions, the primary cooperatives and its members. JCCU is maintaining the contacts with the district, zonal and national government and third parties. Furthermore JCCU initiates, organizes and monitors the investment they are doing on behalf of the members. JCCU plays a role in advocacy and service provision to members; and is positioning itself more and more as a business partner for the cooperative farmers. JCCU recently invested in a Junar processing plant where Junar is processed into juice thereby adding value to the produce of the farmers of the cooperatives. The uniqueness of the federation is believed to be the combination of a partially farmer owned business and fully farmer led federation.

Agriterra has done company assessment of JCCU on 10-15 Feb, 2016. The company assessment team recommended that JCCU fulfill the criteria that Agriterra requires in order to start a client relationship with JCCU The cooperation focused on support in professionalizing JCCU at this stage.

The action plan was started in August 2016 as the starting up phase of a cooperation between JCCU and Agriterra. The 2016 action plan supported JCCU to increase strategic focus of the union through development of a strategic plan, strengthen the financial management, define how JCCU can provide sustainable services to its members and select top 10 PCs and strengthen them on cooperative principles and cooperative governance.

Strengthening JCCU in providing sustainable services to its members.

rows.reporting: Stichting Agriterra
rows.title: support KDDC in improving and upscaling dairy processing
rows.description:
Kamdhenu Dairy Development Cooperative is a farmer led organization in the eastern part of Nepal with a dual membership model. Membership is open for primary cooperatives (45) and also for individuals (230). Members come from the entire supply chain of dairy products: dairy farmers & cooperatives for milk supply, staff members of the cooperative for processing & distribution and retailers for the sale to consumers. About 10.000 (small) dairy farmers are indirect members of the cooperative and deliver milk to the cooperative. The cooperative provides two main services to the members: dairy processing and financial services. Since April 2016 a new Board of Directors and Audit Committee was elected. The daily operation is managed by a management team with both experienced and junior managers. Since April 2016, the previous Finance and administrative officer is taken up the position as interim general manager.The current product portfolio includes milk, yoghurt, ghee, paneer, khoa and butter, selling around average 14.000 litres per day, with some seasonal fluctuations. Production of milk is around 14.000 liters per day, in line with the sales. Market opportunities are good . The capacity of the current factory is 25.000 litres, large enough for the coming 2 years to handle the current production. Aim is to grow in two years time towards 22.000 liters a day. This will require cleaning up and rearranging of the current factory, requiring limited investments only. A clear strategy and focus on the implementation of the strategy will help KDDC to make a gradual grow and get ready for the possible next step: moving and expanding the processing factory after 2 years time.

The activity plan includes Advisory Services only, so no project budget needed. A Letter of Intent will be enough for this first action plan/contract

Support KDDC in preparing a strategic business plan and improve the financial management in order to improve and upscale dairy processing and marketing.

rows.reporting: Stichting Agriterra
rows.title: Improving financial management and services to members
rows.description:
LDMPCUL is a Union of primary Dairy cooperatives in Lalitpur district. After the organisational assesment in March 2016, the organisation is accepted as a potential client of Agriterra. The BHAG of the organisation is to have its own, succesfull processing factory. This is a long term dream and there are several steps to be taken before that.

The mission team recommends to focus the proposed cooperation on: - Identifying and framing the future direction of the federation based on few very clear strategic objectives and goals, by preparing a strategic plan- Organisational strengthening of the union and its member cooperatives in terms of planning and control; financial management and governance. - Improved and financially sustainable services for the members in order to be well prepared for the next (long term) step: starting a processing factory. Access to funds is not the main challenge, but rather to upgrade the milk production, member commitment and strong governance; and improved managerial and financial management skills.



Concrete proposed joint activities for the second half of 2016 are:1. Participation in the financial management training organised by Agriterra (August 2016)2. Prepare an action plan in this workshop and execute this plan, with the aim to further improve the financial management of the union but also to role out the knowledge to primary coops. 3. Prepare a strategic (3-5 years) and 1 year operational plan of the Union, with the option to work out the details of providing (financially) sustainable services to member. This is a process and requires the active input of Board and staff members, and validation with the members.4. Based on the strategic plan, a concrete action plan can be prepared for cooperation with Agriterra.

null

Goal for 2016: 

- improve financial management of LDMPCU - SMART strategic business plan for coming 2 years available in September 2016 and approved by BoD in October 2016- Strengthen LDMPCUL and primary cooperatives capacity in managing agricultural cooperatives- Sustainable services system aiming at improved production in place to be made operational in 2017

rows.reporting: Stichting Agriterra
rows.title: Towards sustainable service provision and effective lobby and advocacy
rows.description:
Nepal Agriculture Cooperative Central Federation Ltd. (NACCFL) is the umbrella organization of Small Farmer Cooperative Limited (SFCL) and similar other agriculture cooperatives. SFCLs are agricultural microfinance cooperatives which are fully owned and managed by the local community. They have their origin in the Small Farmers Development Program (SFDP)which had been implemented by the Agricultural Development Bank Ltd. (ADBL) in 1975. As of Aug 2015, local communities in 650 VDCs have formed their own SFCLs spread in 68 districts throughout the country. Among these SFCLs, 11 SFCLs have been performing limited banking operations with the permission of Nepal Rastra Bank (NRB). The formation of the NACCFL was based on the felt need of all local federations to have a national representation and followed a bottom-up approach. 
Majority of farmers do not have access to information on available (government) support programmes to improve the Agricultural sector, or do not know how to present their needs in a proper way. And vice versa, the government is not closely enough informed about the real farmers needs while preparing the major documents such as agriculture perspective plan, government fifth year plan, policy and procedures, and strategic plan. Because of that, policies are not favorable to the small holders farmers. There is need of farmers consultation while preparing such documents to make them farmers friendly. Thus the present proposal act as bridge between farmers needs and policy.

Objectives: 1. Improve the financial sustainability and services of NACCFL. Aim is that NACCFL can cover its overhead costs by itself within 2 years time. 2. M&E database of member coops is 100% up to date at the end of 2016, The database serves as a source of facts and information to design future interventions, services and can be used on effective lobby and advocacy.3. NACCFL institutionalises FACT methodology in its regular work at national and regional level. outcome: Access to recources , influence on policy proposals and design of porgrammes. 4, Support 10 agri oriented member cooperatives in proper Business plan preparation. 
Activities:
1. Workshop on Farmer Advocacy Consultation Tools I (FACT I)2. Piloting with Farmer Advocacy Consultation Tools 3. Experience sharing Workshop on Farmer Advocacy Consultation Tools II4. ToT on Farmer Advocacy Consultation Tools II (FACT II) for Active Board Members5. Role out FACT 1 Workshop in 5 regions6. M&E volunteers training7. Business planning coaching and trainings

Main goals:  - Lay the foundation for financialy sustainable service provison - Build the capacity of NACCFL representatives at national and district level in effective and systematic lobby and advocacy with the aim to generate funds and have favourable policies for farmers cooperatives. 

Specific goals: 



1. Informing and mobilizing farmers’ organizations about the public program especially government funds that are available at the local, district ad national level. 2. Ensuring FOs’ involvement in the institutional setting of the government policy/program design process especially at the national level: example: input on the new coop law and cop tax systems.3. Farmers’ organizations prepare action plans /proposals for their involvement in the implementation of the programmes/ getting access to certain funds of government programmes.

rows.reporting: Stichting Agriterra
rows.title: Improving the financial sustainability and providing sustainable services to members
rows.description:
SFACFL Dhading is client of Agriterra since 2013. It is a federation of small farmer agricultural cooperatives in Dhading district, with about 42.000 farmers. The federation is member of NACCFL, the APEX organisation of SFACL in Nepal, having about 720 institutional memebrs . 

Main objective of the federation is to support the members in being good cooperatives both in saving and credit as well as in agriculture.The last three years Agriterra supported this federation in expanding its membership base, resulting in the coverage of the whole district. All member SFACL have their own credit and saving system, and when joining the federation, they get also access to credit facilities of the Small Farmers Development Bank, in ownership of all SFACL of Nepal. Hence acces to credit is (to some level) fulfilled.



Agriterra's cooperation focuses more and more on strengthening the Federation in providing good quality services for members to improve the agricultural sector, and do this is a financially sustainable way. The approach is to strengthen the capacity of the so called Lead Farmer: innovating farmers that are open to do new things and dare to take some risks. By training this top layer of farmers and supporting them in playing a model role and coaching role to others, it is expected that more farmers will follow the good examples and will improve their entrepreneurship by better productivity/ better quality and better marketing. Activities will focus on three topics: - record keeping and bench marking at the farm level, - improved soil management techniques and - improved collective marketing/ better access to markets. 



Dhading was heavily affected by the earth quake (april 2015) . The financial situation of the federation is not so healthy right now, as they waived the interest on loans of the member coops, resulting in a direct and long term loss. Hence , the federation would like to become financialy healthy again and has already taken some measures to overcome the problem caused by an emotionally driven decision. Agriterra will further support the federation in improving its financial management and financial sustainability (see special services.)

The activity plan for 2016 focuses on three main objectives:1. Improved Financial management : enhance own income; cover overhead 100% by itself2. Sustainable farmer led service provision focusing on better production and entrepreneurship3. Improved access to quality seeds 

Proposed activities are:AD 1. At the federation level: Financial management training and coachingStrategic and operational plan of SFACFLStrategy in place working towards 100% own overhead cost funding



Ad 2.At the primary cooperatives level (10 SFACL are targeted with this programme): - Role out of recordkeeping at the individual farmers level- Benchmarking training for lead farmers and field staff- Enhancing collective marketing strategies and Improved collective marketing by cooperatives- Improved soil management knowledge: soil testing and soil improvement techniques 



Ad 3. Make a business plan for local seed production for maize, rice etc.- implement the BP-200 farmers are engaged in seed production and at least 1000 farmers have access to quality seedsThe activities were identified during the workshop: sustainable farmer led services. The services are provided by the federation, but lead farmesr and technicians of primary coopertiaves are the key change agents in the field. Services are provided on a cost sharing bases: partly subsidised , partly paid for by the primary coopertiaves and/or the federation

1. Improved Financial management : enhance own income; cover overhead 100% by itself2. Sustainable farmer led service provision focusing on better production and entrepreneurship3. Improved access to quality seeds

rows.reporting: Stichting Agriterra
rows.title: Sustainable service provision to members;  cooperative marketing and improved access to quality seeds
rows.description:
SFACFL Makwanpur (federation of Agricultural cooperatives) promotes commercial farming among its members, focusing on the innovators and lead farmers of the district. Building the entrepreneurial skills of these farmers will set an example for other farmers, showing them practicaly how to produce in a sustainable way for the market. Focus will be on record keeping at the farm level, bench marking through study groups and collective or cooperative marketing. Focus will be on 8 member cooperatives, those having better access to markets. At the same time, the federation will improve its financial position: if farmers realise and feel the benefits of the services provided by the federation, they will start contributing to pay for certain services. The federation aims to improve its internal income generation with at least 20% (compared to last year) this year.

Proposed activities for 2016:

1. Improved Financial sustainability of the federation by offering quality services and developing a business case on seeds- Prepare a businessplan/operational plan on services by the Federation, with earning models related to services- basic financial management training- Propagation and distribution of of improved seed (maize, rice) following a sustainable business plan





2. rows.reporting: Stichting Agriterra
rows.title: Start up Impact@origin progam
rows.description:
Tinjure Tea Cooperative produces high quality orthodox special tea and is seeking access to the European Market. MAAS International is a potential client for this premium tea and willing to invest in this cooperative and its products to create impact add origin: value additional at the cooperative level. This will create a win win situation for all parties; employment and investment opportunities in Nepal, a high quality product that can be exported and a special product for MAAS.
In September 2016, a company assessment was conducted by Agriterra and MAAS. Though Tinjure may not pass criteria of Agriterra, this unique opportunity with MAAS offers an excellent business case and AGriterra can offer its expertise in this trajectory supporting both MAAS and Tinjure. This action plan is related to the last quarter of 2016 and the main objective is to prepare Tinjure for the impact@origin trajectory. Focus will be on improving financial management, explore packaging options, improve quality and set up a track an trace system, business and marketing development, explore the certification options etc.

null

rows.reporting: Stichting Agriterra
rows.title: Cooperative marketing strategies based on commercial farmer members
rows.description:
During the Company assessment in March 2016, it became clear that the cooperative has an enthusiastic, young and well educated board members, financially healthy operation, solid track record on management and governance, focus on cooperative marketing and larger number of farmer members. The team realized that the cooperative need external support in developing the cooperative marketing strategies. Thus, the team recommended Agriterra for cooperation with the cooperative initially for six months and develop elaborate action plan after outcome from six months cooperation.

The proposed project activities for half year are to collect and interpret information form farmer mapping, market survey and possible product diversification, and conduct research different market propositions. Based upon the information of farmers and market, develop an cooperative marketing strategy with a  financial business case. The project also support the cooperative in developing financial management capacities, identify and prioritize farmers needs, and develop market strategies.

The AACL made the first steps in the cooperative marketing the last years but without a clearly defined business plan. This resulted in the agro shop that sells small quantities of products mainly produced by the subsistence farmers. The shop is not sufficient to sell all the products produced by the members. At this moment there is no cooperative marketing for the commercial farmers. The AACL has 2015 members out of which 150 are commercial farmers. 

The project will focus on the commercial farmers for the pilot phase and include the subsistence farmers afterwards. The main commodities for the cooperative marketing is seasonal vegetables produced by the members. The objective of the project is to develop the suitable and potential collective output marketing strategies in order to have their commercial member farmers benefit from higher prices that can be achieved further in the supply chain or through different market outlets. The tentative budget for this project is Euro 4500. Agriterra and AACL will monitor and evaluate the progress of the project. Based upon the six months cooperation, the AACL will develop and implement the business which would be sustainable regular income for the cooperative.

Develop a cooperative marketing strategy which gives more marketing opportunities and better prices for all their members.

rows.reporting: Stichting Agriterra
rows.title: Increasing volume of processed products through improved marketing plans and skills
rows.description:
Annapurna Dairy Producer Cooperative Limited(ADPCL) is mainly   specialized in the collection,  chilling, processing and marketing of the milk   produced by its 484 farmer members. The cooperative furthermore has its own   feed factory, model dairy farm, and provides to its members credit and saving   services. On top of that the cooperative recently bought a piece of land   where it plans to develop a larger dairy processing facility. However, this   ambition has not been further explored as ADPCL currently has difficulties in   using the available processing capacity of its factory. Processing is   required in order to add value to the milk of their members. Nowadays, many   members do not further invest in expanding their dairy business as it is   perceived to not be profitable. Milk intake from members is no longer   increasing and remains at approximately 4000 liters per day of which 2000   liters is processed. This creates a significant threat for the cooperative.   In order to remain competitive the cooperative needs to grow and therefore   members need to produce more milk. The BHAG developed with ADPCL is:   Improving profitability of the cooperative and the profitability of dairy   farming for the members through increasing the volume of processed dairy   products from 2000 liters today to 4000 liters in 2 years time. The biggest   challenge ADPCL is experiencing in realizing this ambition is lack of   marketing of processed products. Processing capacity is available in the   current facility, but it is not used to its optimum because the cooperative   cannot find sufficient market. products. Processing capacity is available in the current facility, but it is
not used to its optimum because the cooperative cannot find sufficient market.

strengthening the marketing and organizational capacity of the organization.
